proposals.current_explanation: Gusinsky is the 1990s oligarch most associated with independent Russian television — NTV under Evgeny Kiselyov was the first Soviet/Russian news channel to cover conflict (First Chechen War) and Kremlin scandals with real independence. proposals.current_correct_text: A large accumulation of household ruble balances that could not be matched by available consumer goods at fixed state prices — producing empty shelves and long lines rather than open inflation, and storing up purchasing power that would surge into spending the moment prices were freed
proposals.proposed_correct_text: Excess household ruble balances unmatched by goods at fixed prices — producing queues rather than open inflation, with pent-up demand waiting for liberalization
proposals.current_explanation: Monetary overhang is a classic late-socialist phenomenon and a key concept for understanding why price liberalization produced such severe one-time inflation. By 1990-91 Soviet households held ruble balances roughly equal to a year's worth of retail turnover, but state stores had few goods at their fixed prices. When Gaidar freed prices in January 1992, the overhang converted into a threefold price jump within weeks — a classic 'open inflation replacing repressed inflation' episode.

proposals.current_explanation: The October 1990 compromise was the last serious all-union attempt at a coordinated transition program. Shatalin-Yavlinsky had proposed rapid stabilization and privatization over 500 days; Ryzhkov-Abalkin (the Council of Ministers' plan) proposed a slower government-led transition over 5-6 years. Gorbachev, caught between republics pushing for more radical reform and his own centrist institutional instincts, endorsed a blended Presidential Program that neither side found credible. Yeltsin then had the RSFSR Supreme Soviet formally adopt the 500 Days plan for Russia — setting up the republic-center collision that became decisive in 1991.

proposals.current_correct_text: A letter of 24 December 1991 from Boris Yeltsin to the UN Secretary-General — endorsed by the other CIS states via the 21 December Alma-Ata Declaration — asserting that Russia was assuming USSR membership (including the permanent Security Council seat and veto) as the 'continuing state' rather than as a new member
proposals.proposed_correct_text: A 24 December 1991 letter from Yeltsin to the UN Secretary-General — endorsed by the 21 December Alma-Ata Declaration — claiming the USSR's seat as the 'continuing state'
proposals.current_explanation: The 'continuing state' framing was a deliberate legal choice with major consequences. It allowed Russia to inherit not just the UN Security Council seat but also the USSR's treaty obligations (including START I, CFE, INF), its embassies and diplomatic property abroad, and its external debt. The other thirteen (non-Baltic) successor states joined the UN separately as new members (Ukraine and Belarus retained their 1945 UN seats), accepted their share of Soviet assets through CIS agreements, and implicitly agreed to the asymmetric 'continuing state' status. This framing also preserved the Russian nuclear arsenal under a single command, a concern shared by Washington at the time.
proposals.rationale: Trimmed answer to 27 words; moved the seat-and-veto detail and broader inheritance to the explanation.

proposals.current_correct_text: Ukrainian arrears for Russian gas imports, Ukrainian informal off-take from transit pipelines to Europe, and periodic Russian threats of supply cuts — issues that would escalate into the full-scale cutoffs of 2006 and 2009 but were already structural features of the 1990s trade
proposals.proposed_correct_text: Ukrainian arrears for Russian gas, Ukrainian off-take from transit pipelines to Europe, and periodic Russian threats of supply cuts
proposals.current_explanation: Ukrainian gas issues are one of the main transit-state political-economy problems of the 1990s. Ukraine's thermal power and heavy industry depended on Russian gas; payment capacity was limited; Gazprom ran up large receivables; and transit arrangements were shot through with disputes over Ukrainian off-take from the export pipeline system. Through the 1990s these were typically resolved through renegotiated debt-for-shares swaps, barter arrangements, and price discounts. The pattern crystallized into open supply-cut crises in 2006 and 2009, and was a central driver of Russian investment in alternative pipeline routes (Nord Stream, Turkstream).
proposals.rationale: Trimmed answer to 19 words; moved 2006/2009 cutoffs and structural detail to the explanation.

proposals.current_correct_text: Unilateral authority to issue decrees with the force of law (provided they do not contradict the Constitution or federal statutes), to nominate the Prime Minister (subject to Duma confirmation), and to dissolve the Duma if it rejects the president's PM nominee three times
proposals.proposed_correct_text: Authority to issue decrees with the force of law, nominate the prime minister, and dissolve the Duma if it rejects the PM three times
proposals.current_explanation: The 1993 Constitution's 'super-presidential' design is anchored in three levers: wide decree authority (Article 90), control of the PM nominating process (Article 111) with the dissolution threat (if the Duma rejects the same or successive nominees three times, the Duma is dissolved), and de facto control over the Security Council and foreign policy. This architecture was drafted in the shadow of the October 1993 crisis — deliberately preventing a repeat of the Supreme Soviet standoff — and became the institutional foundation on which Putin later consolidated power.

proposals.current_explanation: The institutional lineage matters for the 1993 constitutional crisis. During the Soviet period each union republic had its own Supreme Soviet; the RSFSR's parliament sat in a building on the Moscow River that came to be called the White House (completed 1981). In 1990 the RSFSR reorganized along Gorbachev's earlier federal model, creating a large Congress of People's Deputies that elected a smaller Supreme Soviet from its own ranks. After the Soviet collapse, this structure persisted as the 'Supreme Soviet of the Russian Federation.' Under Khasbulatov, the standing Supreme Soviet and the wider Congress became the institutional base of Yeltsin's opponents. The October 1993 crisis ended with Yeltsin's Decree 1400 dissolving both bodies and with the December 1993 referendum replacing them with the Federal Assembly (Duma + Federation Council) established by the new Constitution.

Banks (Menatep, Oneksim, Alfa, and others) lent to the government with shares in Yukos, Norilsk Nickel, Sidanko, and similar firms as collateral; auctions were rigged so lending banks won, and a small, politically connected oligarchy acquired the commanding heights of the economy at very low valuations. The episode underpins the argument that Russia's 1990s reform produced 'oligarchic capitalism' rather than competitive markets, and it became a defining target of Putin's later 'equidistance' doctrine vis-à-vis oligarchs.

proposals.current_correct_text: The Russian Central Bank's surprise July 1993 decision to invalidate all Soviet-era and pre-1993-design ruble notes on Russian soil — forcing other republics, which still circulated those notes, either to adopt Russian-issued new notes under Russian terms or to launch their own currencies
proposals.proposed_correct_text: The Russian Central Bank's surprise July 1993 decision to invalidate Soviet-era and pre-1993-design ruble notes on Russian soil, forcing other republics to choose between Russia's new notes and their own currencies
proposals.current_explanation: The ruble zone's collapse illustrates how monetary policy can force political separation. Through 1992 several CIS republics kept old ruble notes circulating, giving them a share in what was effectively Russian seigniorage. In July 1993 the Russian CBR under Geraschenko abruptly announced that only new 1993-design ruble notes would be valid on Russian soil, with very short exchange windows. Non-Russian holders of old notes faced value destruction. Within months, Ukraine deepened its use of the karbovanets, Belarus accelerated plans for a national ruble, the Baltic states (already outside the ruble zone) consolidated their separate currencies, and Moldova introduced the leu. The ruble zone as a multi-republic monetary bloc was effectively over by the end of 1993.

proposals.current_explanation: Neformaly (from the Russian for 'informal [groups]') are a pivotal category for understanding the Soviet collapse. proposals.current_correct_text: The first partially competitive legislative elections in Soviet Ukraine: the Democratic Bloc (anchored by Rukh) took roughly a quarter of the seats while Communists retained roughly three-quarters, and reform-minded Communist Leonid Kravchuk was then elected chairman of the new parliament

proposals.current_explanation: The March 1990 Ukrainian SSR parliamentary elections are a key inflection point in Ukraine's divergence from Russia. The Democratic Bloc — a coalition anchored by Rukh, the People's Movement of Ukraine for Perestroika — won roughly a quarter of the seats, concentrated heavily in western Ukraine. The Communist Party retained the majority, but its ranks included reformist currents, and the new parliament elected Leonid Kravchuk (a senior CPSU ideology secretary) as its chairman. Over the next 18 months, that parliament drove the 16 July 1990 Declaration of State Sovereignty, the response to the August 1991 coup, the 24 August 1991 Act of Independence, and the scheduling of the December 1991 independence referendum — all with Kravchuk, not a Rukh outsider, at the helm.

proposals.current_explanation: This split frames post-Soviet Russian foreign policy. A realist reading emphasizes that Russia's reduced material capabilities after 1991 (shrunken economy, depleted military, lost territory) should have driven accommodation with a stronger West, while its still-substantial assets (nuclear arsenal, UN veto, size) gave it leverage to resist encirclement. A liberal reading emphasizes domestic political institutions — whether Russia was consolidating as a democracy, how its norms aligned with the West — and invokes claims like the democratic peace (democracies rarely fight each other). Either framework generates ambiguous and sometimes contradictory predictions for 1990s Russia, which is part of why debates over NATO enlargement, the war in Kosovo, and Russia's eventual foreign-policy turn remain contested.
proposals.rationale: Trimmed answer to 21 words; preserves the realism-vs-liberalism contrast and democratic-peace anchor.

proposals.proposed_correct_text: Poland (24 August 1989) → Hungary (September-October) → East Germany (9 November) → Czechoslovakia (late November) → Romania (Ceauşescu executed 25 December)
proposals.current_explanation: The sequence matters because each transition cued the next. Poland's Round Table (February-April 1989) produced partially-free elections on 4 June and the Mazowiecki government on 24 August — the first non-communist prime minister in Eastern Europe since 1948. Hungary's fence-opening (June cutting ceremony; full opening to Austrian border 11 September) allowed East German refugees through, draining the GDR and making Honecker's regime unsustainable. Schabowski's 9 November press conference sent the Berlin Wall down. Czechoslovakia's Velvet Revolution (17-29 November) was peaceful. Only Romania's transition (21-25 December 1989) was violent, ending in Ceauşescu's execution. The cascade was tightly chained, with Gorbachev's non-intervention stance (the Sinatra Doctrine) supplying the permissive condition.

proposals.current_correct_text: Gorbachev used it to push through a sweeping program of political reform — creating the Congress of People's Deputies, introducing contested elections, and shifting real authority from party organs toward newly empowered soviets
proposals.proposed_correct_text: Gorbachev used it to push through political reform — creating the Congress of People's Deputies, introducing contested elections, and shifting authority from party organs toward newly empowered soviets
proposals.current_explanation: The 19th Party Conference was where Gorbachev laid out the plan for a new legislative architecture: a Congress of People's Deputies elected with contested races and a standing Supreme Soviet drawn from it. This was the first time a major political reform was publicly debated at a national party forum with live media coverage (a glasnost innovation), and the resulting constitutional amendments in late 1988 produced the legislature that would convene in 1989.

proposals.current_correct_text: A centrally set plan of output targets for heavy industry that launched Stalin's crash industrialization and was paired with forced agricultural collectivization to extract the resources to fund it
proposals.proposed_correct_text: A centrally set plan of heavy-industry output targets that launched Stalin's crash industrialization, paired with forced collectivization to extract the resources to fund it
proposals.current_explanation: The First Five-Year Plan was the concrete instrument through which Stalin's 'Revolution from Above' was carried out. It set ambitious output targets for coal, steel, machinery, and electrification; it was paired with forced collectivization to squeeze grain out of the countryside and feed the industrial workforce; and it was declared complete after four years. The plan inaugurated a pattern — successive five-year plans, dominance of heavy industry, output-over-quality metrics — that defined the Soviet command economy until its collapse.
proposals.rationale: Trimmed answer to 24 words; preserves heavy-industry + crash-industrialization + collectivization-pairing core.

proposals.current_correct_text: A nationwide network of forced-labor camps administered by the NKVD that held millions of prisoners sentenced on political and criminal charges and supplied coerced labor for mining, logging, canal-building, and other industrial projects
proposals.proposed_correct_text: A nationwide network of NKVD-administered forced-labor camps that held millions on political and criminal charges and supplied coerced labor for mining, logging, and canal-building
proposals.current_explanation: The Russian acronym GULag (Main Administration of Corrective Labor Camps and Colonies) names both the bureaucracy that ran the camps and, by extension, the camp system itself. At peak Stalinist scale, roughly 3.6 million were under NKVD jurisdiction with about 1.36 million in the camps proper. The system did three things at once: it removed perceived political enemies from society, it supplied forced labor for heavy-industry and infrastructure projects in hard-to-populate regions, and — through its sheer scale and arbitrariness — it produced the atmosphere of fear on which Stalinist totalitarianism relied. Solzhenitsyn's literary treatment later turned 'Gulag' into a global synonym for Soviet repression.
proposals.rationale: Trimmed answer to 25 words; trimmed 'and other industrial projects' tail.
